title: Styling Signs
subtitle: The style string as light markup for customization and rich presentation
summary: The style string as optional light markup for customization and rich presentation.
synopsis: The style string deserves focused technical treatment. This entry explains it as optional light markup for customizing sign presentation, clarifies its relation to rendering, and argues against treating styling as part of canonical plain-text encoding.

slides.title: Styling Is a Separate Layer
slides.summary: The style string belongs to the presentation layer rather than the canonical encoding layer.

image_prompts.concrete: Scholarly technical slide showing one encoded sign with attached style string and a styled output preview, clean white background
image_prompts.abstract: One stable sign with a light customization halo surrounding it

slides.speech: The style string matters, but it should not be mistaken for the core text model. It is better understood as optional light markup for customizing presentation. Giving it its own entry lets us define it carefully without blurring the distinction between canonical encoding and rendered output.
slides.notes: Make the markup analogy explicit and careful.

slides.title: Whole-Sign Styling
slides.summary: The style string can describe whole-sign appearance without redefining the underlying sign text.

image_prompts.concrete: Technical styling slide showing one sign with padding, color, and scale variations controlled by a style string, clear white background
image_prompts.abstract: One stable sign wearing several carefully controlled visual treatments

slides.speech: One of the strengths of the style string is that it can customize the whole sign while leaving the underlying text intact. Whole-sign padding, size, colors, and class and id hooks affect presentation, but they do not change what sign text is being represented.
slides.notes: Keep the distinction between content and appearance explicit.

slides.title: Symbol-Level Styling
slides.summary: The style string can also target particular symbols inside a sign for finer visual control.

image_prompts.concrete: Instructional slide highlighting individual symbols inside one sign with color overrides from a style string, academic technical layout
image_prompts.abstract: A single sign with selected symbols gently illuminated for emphasis

slides.speech: The style string is not limited to whole-sign effects. It can also target individual symbols inside a sign for color emphasis. That makes it useful for teaching, annotation, visual emphasis, and interface design, while still keeping those concerns separate from canonical text encoding.
slides.notes: Stress utility without letting it become part of the core model.

slides.title: Keep Styling Out of Canonical Text
slides.summary: Separating styling from encoding and rendering keeps the technical architecture clean and teachable.
bullets: A separate layer with its own semantics
slides.speech: The style string deserves its own entry precisely because it is easy to misplace. If it is treated as part of the encoding, the architecture becomes muddy. If it is buried in rendering alone, its semantics stay under-explained. The clean solution is to treat it as optional light markup with its own definition.
slides.notes: This is the decisive architectural slide.

image_prompts.concrete: Boundary slide separating canonical FSW and SWU text from optional style strings, markup, colors, and presentation customization
image_prompts.abstract: Canonical text protected from presentation markup by a clear technical boundary
